use_cases.description: Keeping calendar and contact data fully in-house to meet data-residency or privacy requirements.

use_cases.title: Group Meeting Scheduling
use_cases.description: Using free/busy lookups and shared calendar delegation to coordinate meetings across a team.

enrichment.short_description: DaVinci Resolve is Blackmagic Design's professional video editing, color grading, visual effects, and audio post-production software, free with a paid Studio upgrade.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (free version + one-time perpetual license upgrade)
languages: and multiple other languages via localized UI
enrichment.seo_title: DaVinci Resolve 2026: Free vs Studio Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DaVinci Resolve review 2026: free editing, color grading, Fusion VFX, and Fairlight audio, plus what the $295 Studio license adds over the free version.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: DaVinci Resolve is a professional video editing, color correction, visual effects, and audio post-production application developed by Blackmagic Design, an Australian company founded on September 7, 2001 by Grant Petty and headquartered in South Melbourne, Victoria. Blackmagic Design initially built its business around video capture and playback hardware, releasing its first product, the DeckLink capture card, in 2002, before expanding into broadcast switchers, cameras, and post-production software through a series of strategic acquisitions. The company's most consequential software acquisition came in 2009, when it purchased da Vinci Systems, the maker of the industry-standard color-grading software from which DaVinci Resolve takes its name and its original core color-correction technology; Blackmagic subsequently expanded the product far beyond color grading by acquiring eyeon Software in 2014, bringing in the Fusion visual effects and compositing engine that now powers Resolve's Fusion page, and Fairlight in 2016, an audio technology company whose console and mixing technology now powers Resolve's dedicated Fairlight audio post-production page. The result is a single application organized into distinct 'pages,' each dedicated to a stage of the post-production workflow: a Cut and Edit page for non-linear video editing, a Color page for professional color grading and correction, a Fusion page for visual effects and motion graphics compositing, a Fairlight page for multi-track audio mixing and sound design, and a Deliver page for exporting finished projects in a wide range of formats and codecs, all sharing the same underlying project and media, which removes the need to move footage between separate editing, color, VFX, and audio applications as is common with competing workflows built around Adobe Premiere Pro, After Effects, and Audition as separate programs. DaVinci Resolve's pricing model is unusual in the professional creative software industry: the base version of the application is entirely free, with no watermark, no time limit, and no subscription requirement, and it supports professional editing, color grading, visual effects, and audio work up to Ultra HD (3840x2160) resolution at up to 60 frames per second, running on Windows, macOS, and Linux. A paid upgrade, DaVinci Resolve Studio, is available as a one-time perpetual license costing 295 US dollars, which, unlike subscription-based competitors such as Adobe Premiere Pro or Avid Media Composer, requires no recurring payment and includes all future feature updates at no additional cost once purchased. Studio unlocks the DaVinci Neural Engine, Blackmagic's suite of AI-powered tools for tasks like automatic speed warping, facial recognition and tracking, voice isolation, and Magic Mask object isolation; additional Resolve FX effects; temporal and AI-based spatial noise reduction; text-based editing that lets editors cut video by editing a transcript; film grain and optical blur simulation tools; support for resolutions up to 32K and frame rates up to 120 frames per second; multi-GPU support for real-time playback of high-bitrate, 10-bit professional formats; and Blackmagic Cloud-based remote collaboration features that let distributed teams work on the same project simultaneously. Studio licenses are also bundled for free with the purchase of many Blackmagic Design hardware products, such as its cinema cameras and capture devices, which has historically been a major distribution channel for the paid version. DaVinci Resolve's most direct competitors are Adobe Premiere Pro, the long-standing subscription-based industry-standard editor bundled within Adobe Creative Cloud; Apple's Final Cut Pro, a one-time-purchase editor available only on macOS; and Avid Media Composer, which remains dominant in traditional film and broadcast editing workflows, particularly in Hollywood post-production houses. DaVinci Resolve differentiates itself primarily on cost, given that its core functionality is free and its paid tier is a one-time purchase rather than a subscription, and on its integrated, single-application workflow spanning editing, color, VFX, and audio, whereas Adobe's ecosystem achieves similar breadth through several separate applications (Premiere Pro, After Effects, Audition) that must be licensed together. Historically, DaVinci Resolve's color-grading tools have been considered the industry benchmark, used on a large share of major Hollywood feature films and television productions, which has driven adoption among colorists even at studios that use a different application for the actual editing. The tool's target users span a very wide range, from hobbyist YouTube and social media video creators using the free version, to independent filmmakers and small production studios, to professional colorists, VFX artists, and audio engineers working on feature films, television, and commercials who rely on the Studio version's advanced tools. Blackmagic Design, the parent company, generates the bulk of its revenue from selling professional broadcast and cinema hardware, such as cameras, capture cards, and video switchers, which allows it to treat DaVinci Resolve's free tier as a strategic tool for driving adoption of its broader hardware and software ecosystem rather than as a primary standalone revenue source, a business model that is a significant departure from purely software-subscription-based competitors. This is a major reason DaVinci Resolve has been able to sustain a genuinely capable free tier for well over a decade without introducing usage limits, watermarks, or feature expirations that force casual or hobbyist users into a paid plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DaVinci Resolve is Blackmagic Design's all-in-one professional video editing, color grading, visual effects, and audio post-production application. Blackmagic Design was founded in 2001 by Grant Petty and acquired da Vinci Systems' color-grading technology in 2009, later adding Fusion VFX (2014) and Fairlight audio (2016) tools into the same application.

The software is organized into dedicated pages for editing, color, Fusion visual effects, Fairlight audio, and delivery, all sharing the same project and media so teams avoid moving footage between separate applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The free version supports professional editing, color grading, VFX, and audio work up to Ultra HD at 60fps on Windows, macOS, and Linux, with no watermark or time limit.

The paid Studio version adds the DaVinci Neural Engine's AI tools, additional Resolve FX, noise reduction, text-based editing, up to 32K resolution and 120fps support, multi-GPU playback, and Blackmagic Cloud collaboration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: DaVinci Resolve's base version is completely free with full professional functionality. DaVinci Resolve Studio is a one-time perpetual license costing $295, with no subscription and all future updates included.
Studio licenses are also bundled free with many Blackmagic Design hardware purchases, such as cinema cameras and capture devices.

features.title: Non-Linear Video Editing
features.description: A full-featured Cut and Edit page for professional video editing workflows.

features.title: Professional Color Grading
features.description: Industry-benchmark color correction tools inherited from the original da Vinci Systems color technology.

features.title: Fusion Visual Effects
features.description: Node-based compositing and motion graphics tools integrated directly into the same application.

features.title: Fairlight Audio Post-Production
features.description: A full multi-track audio mixing and sound design suite built from Blackmagic's Fairlight audio technology.

features.title: DaVinci Neural Engine (Studio)
features.description: AI-powered tools for facial recognition, voice isolation, Magic Mask object isolation, and speed warping.

features.title: Text-Based Editing (Studio)
features.description: Lets editors cut footage by editing an automatically generated transcript.

features.title: High-Resolution, High-Frame-Rate Support (Studio)
features.description: Supports up to 32K resolution and 120fps playback with multi-GPU acceleration.

features.title: Blackmagic Cloud Collaboration (Studio)
features.description: Enables distributed teams to collaborate remotely on the same project in real time.

pros: Free version offers genuinely professional-grade editing, color, VFX, and audio tools with no watermark or time limit
Studio upgrade is a one-time $295 purchase, not a recurring subscription
Single integrated application replaces the need for separate editing, color, VFX, and audio programs
Industry-benchmark color grading tools used widely in professional film and television post-production
cons: Steeper learning curve than simpler consumer video editors
Some advanced AI and high-resolution features are locked behind the paid Studio license
Can be demanding on hardware, particularly GPU, for smooth performance on complex timelines
Collaboration and cloud features are more limited or absent in the free version

faqs.question: Is DaVinci Resolve really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the base version of DaVinci Resolve is completely free with no watermark, no time limit, and no subscription, supporting professional editing, color grading, VFX, and audio up to Ultra HD at 60fps.

faqs.question: How much does DaVinci Resolve Studio cost?
faqs.answer: DaVinci Resolve Studio is a one-time perpetual license costing $295, with no recurring subscription fees and all future updates included.

faqs.question: What is the difference between DaVinci Resolve free and Studio?
faqs.answer: Studio adds the AI-powered DaVinci Neural Engine, additional Resolve FX, noise reduction, text-based editing, resolutions up to 32K, frame rates up to 120fps, multi-GPU support, and Blackmagic Cloud collaboration.

faqs.question: Who makes DaVinci Resolve?
faqs.answer: DaVinci Resolve is made by Blackmagic Design, an Australian company founded in 2001 by Grant Petty, which acquired the original da Vinci Systems color technology in 2009.

faqs.question: What operating systems does DaVinci Resolve support?
faqs.answer: DaVinci Resolve runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

faqs.question: How does DaVinci Resolve compare to Adobe Premiere Pro?
faqs.answer: DaVinci Resolve offers a free tier and a one-time-purchase Studio upgrade, while Premiere Pro requires an ongoing Creative Cloud subscription; DaVinci Resolve also integrates editing, color, VFX, and audio in one app rather than separate applications.

use_cases.title: Independent Filmmaking and Content Creation

use_cases.audience: YouTubers, social media creators, and independent filmmakers

use_cases.title: Professional Color Grading
use_cases.description: Using industry-standard color correction tools on feature films, television, and commercials.

use_cases.description: Handling editing, VFX compositing, and audio mixing in a single application without exporting between programs.

features: Full editing, color, VFX, and audio tools
